output:
ARBITER (Arbitration of Reward-model Tension via Inferred per-prompt disagreEment Routing) — an inference-time, label-free, per-prompt arbitration layer on the helpfulness↔safety reward-model conflict that Llama 2's two-reward-model design (Touvron et al. 2023, arXiv:2307.09288) exposes but never measures. Epistemic key: [established] = in source/standard literature; [conjectured] = hypothesized, to be measured; [provable-by-construction] = follows from the formula/config nesting; [target-measured] = empirical target to be measured, not asserted.

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can a per-prompt, label-free estimate of the disagreement between an RLHF system's separate helpfulness and safety reward models — calibrated on a small held-out oracle subset and used to replace a single global tradeoff weight λ with a measured per-prompt arbitration decision (answer / safe-complete / clarify / refuse-with-reason) — measurably improve the joint helpful∧safe risk–coverage curve over a fixed-λ RLHF policy, simultaneously reducing both over-refusal on benign prompts and unsafe-compliance on adversarial prompts?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

**The tension ARBITER targets is one Llama 2 itself names as a first-order design problem.** Llama 2 reports [established] that a *single* reward model "struggles to be both helpful and safe," and so trains **two separate reward models** and certifies alignment with **two separately-averaged aggregate win-rates** (mean helpfulness, mean safety) on **disjoint** prompt sets (Touvron et al. 2023, arXiv:2307.09288). That design decision is the strongest possible evidence that the helpfulness↔safety tension is real and unresolved at the modeling level. But the certification stops at the marginals, while **every deployed response must be jointly helpful ∧ safe on the same single prompt**. The gap between "wins both averages" and "is jointly correct on this prompt" — the Marginal-Frontier Gap — is exactly the surface a product LLM is judged on in the wild. Making that joint, per-prompt frontier a *measured* object, rather than an unstated consequence of two independent averages, is the contribution; it is interesting precisely because the field's flagship open-RLHF system advertises the two-RM split yet never reports where the two RMs *disagree*.

**Over-refusal is a real, quantified usability tax — not a hypothetical.** The community has built dedicated benchmarks because exaggerated safety is a measurable failure mode: XSTest (Röttger et al. 2023, arXiv:2308.01263) and OR-Bench (Cui et al. 2024, arXiv:2405.20947) exist [established] specifically to document models refusing **benign** prompts. Symmetrically, adversarial red-teaming suites — HarmBench (Mazeika et al. 2024, arXiv:2402.04249), AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023, UNVERIFIED ID) — document **unsafe-compliance** on adversarial prompts. ARBITER's importance is that these two harms are not independent: both [conjectured] concentrate in the **same contested region** where the helpfulness RM and the safety RM disagree. A method that localizes that region per-prompt addresses **both** the safety failure (unsafe-compliance) **and** the helpfulness/usability failure (over-refusal) with one signal, rather than trading one against the other via a global knob.

**Reframing alignment evaluation from marginal aggregates to per-prompt joint frontiers is a durable methodological shift.** [conjectured] If a paper establishes that mean-helpfulness and mean-safety can both improve while the **joint** helpful∧safe risk–coverage curve stagnates or regresses, it reframes how RLHF systems are reported: the joint AURC / risk–coverage curve becomes the headline, and the **RM-disagreement region becomes a first-class object** to be measured, audited, and routed on — not noise to be averaged away. This is the same "promote-a-scalar-to-a-field" lever applied to spatial (diffusion) and evaluation (faithfulness) axes, now applied to the **alignment-tradeoff axis**: a single global tradeoff weight λ → a measured per-prompt arbitration field λ̂(x). The lever is attractive to future work because it is **inference-time and training-free**: any stack with two scoring heads — two RMs, or a reward model plus a cost/safety model (Safe RLHF, Dai et al. 2023, arXiv:2310.12773), or helpfulness reward plus a constitutional critic (Bai et al. 2022, arXiv:2212.08073) — can adopt the disagreement-routing layer **without re-training a single balanced RM**. It **complements rather than replaces RLHF**: λ̂(x) ≡ λ provably recovers the fixed-λ policy [provable-by-construction], so the method is a strict superset that degrades to today's behavior in the worst case.

**Practical deployment value: safer shipping without retraining, plus an audit surface.** [conjectured/target-measured] Because the arbitration field is computed from frozen-RM signals at inference and calibrated only on a small held-out oracle subset, ARBITER offers a **training-free safety lever** a product team can drop in front of an already-aligned model: it can lower unsafe-compliance on adversarial prompts **and** lower over-refusal on benign prompts at a chosen operating point on the joint risk–coverage curve, with the richer action set (answer / safe-complete / clarify / refuse-with-reason) giving graceful options between "comply" and "hard refuse." The disagreement field doubles as an **auditing map of where alignment is brittle** — the high-λ̂ region is exactly where annotation, red-teaming, or human escalation should be concentrated, directly serving Llama 2's stated **transparency and reproducibility** goals (open weights, documented method). Routing to clarification or human escalation in the contested region is a deployable, accountable alternative to silently picking a global tradeoff that is mis-specified for most individual prompts.

**Scope honesty (standing caveat).** Improvements are [target-measured] on the joint risk–coverage curve against a fixed-λ RLHF comparator, never against an unaligned base model; statistical/empirical improvement is **not** a safety certification and does not bound failure under arbitrary distribution shift; and Llama 2's **English-only**, evaluated-taxonomy limitation carries over — the joint-frontier claims are scoped to the evaluated languages and harm taxonomy, with disaggregated per-category reporting required and non-English / out-of-taxonomy behavior left uncharacterized.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

ARBITER asks whether a **per-prompt, label-free** estimate of helpfulness↔safety reward-model disagreement, calibrated on a small held-out oracle subset, can replace a single global tradeoff weight λ with a measured per-prompt arbitration decision (answer / safe-complete / clarify / refuse-with-reason) and **simultaneously** reduce over-refusal on benign prompts and unsafe-compliance on adversarial prompts. Seven structural difficulties make this hard, and explain why the obvious approaches fail.

**1. The JOINT helpful∧safe frontier is never directly observed.** [established] Llama 2 certifies alignment with **two separately-averaged marginal aggregates** — a mean helpfulness win-rate and a mean safety win-rate — measured on **DISJOINT** prompt sets. But every deployed response must be **jointly** helpful ∧ safe on the **same single prompt**. There is no benchmark that supplies, per prompt, a ground-truth "this response was both helpful and safe" label at deployment scale: helpfulness suites (AlpacaEval, MT-Bench, HH-RLHF helpful split) and safety suites (HarmBench, AdvBench, HH-RLHF harmless split) are constructed for different prompt populations. So the very quantity ARBITER targets — the per-prompt conjunction — is **latent**, observable only on a small, deliberately-constructed oracle subset, and must be *estimated* everywhere else. This is the core obstruction: we are predicting a target whose labels are absent precisely where we need to act.

**2. RM disagreement ≠ error.** [established→conjectured] High conflict between the helpfulness RM r_h and the safety RM r_s does **not** deterministically imply a bad response; many high-conflict prompts are still answered well. Disagreement is at best a **noisy correlate** of joint failure, so a raw conflict signal cannot be used as a decision rule directly — it must be **calibrated** into a measured reliability (P[joint-failure | signal]). Worse, the calibrator must **transfer under distribution shift**: the relationship between RM-conflict and joint-failure on benign prompts (XSTest-style) differs from that on adversarial/jailbreak prompts (HarmBench-style). A calibrator fit on one regime that silently fails to transfer to another would produce confidently-wrong arbitration exactly in the contested region. [conjectured] We therefore expect calibration drift across {benign, adversarial, jailbreak} to be the dominant failure mode of a naive single-calibrator design — which is why the approach (Q5) fits a **regime-stratified** calibrator and reports an explicit calibration-transfer metric.

**3. Safety asymmetry breaks the symmetric-tradeoff framing.** [established] Unsafe-compliance on an adversarial prompt is far costlier than over-refusal on a benign one. A single global λ — and any symmetric risk–coverage objective — implicitly prices both errors on one scale. ARBITER's arbitration objective must encode an **asymmetric loss** (a higher penalty on unsafe-compliance), which means the operating-point selection, the refuse/clarify thresholds, and the risk–coverage curve itself must be defined over an asymmetric cost, not a symmetric trade. Getting this asymmetry wrong collapses ARBITER toward either always-refuse (safe but useless) or always-answer (helpful but unsafe).

**4. The contested region is small and imbalanced.** [established→conjectured] Most prompts are **easy-helpful** (clearly benign, clearly answerable) or **easy-unsafe** (clearly harmful, clearly refusable). The region where r_h and r_s genuinely conflict — where over-refusal and unsafe-compliance both live — is a **small, class-imbalanced minority**. Estimating a reliable per-prompt signal there is statistically hard: the oracle calibration subset must over-sample the contested region without becoming unrepresentative, and any aggregate metric (overall accuracy, mean win-rate) will be **dominated by the easy majority** and blind to contested-region gains. This is why the headline metric must be a **joint risk–coverage curve**, not an average.

**5. Placebo-leakage risk.** [established — playbook lesson] Because arbitration changes the **refusal/clarify RATE**, a *random* arbitration field that merely matches that rate could appear to improve joint metrics for the wrong reason (it shifts coverage, not per-prompt discrimination). If the placebo is shuffled inconsistently — e.g., the random field is fed to the action-selector but the *real* calibrated thresholds or the *real* estimator are left in place anywhere downstream — signal leaks through the un-shuffled term and the placebo spuriously "helps." The placebo must therefore be shuffled **CONSISTENTLY across EVERY term it feeds**: the estimator d(x), all routing thresholds, the action-selection map, AND the calibrator h. (This is the placebo-consistent-shuffle lesson reinforced across prior tasks: shuffle everywhere the field is consumed, never one term.)

**6. Defining a non-circular oracle.** [established — playbook lesson] The oracle's per-prompt "joint helpful∧safe" label must be **defensible** and, critically, must use a labeling scheme **DISTINCT from the cheap RM-gap signal** that drives d(x). If the oracle target were itself derived from the same r_h/r_s gap, the calibration "validation" would pass **trivially** by self-correlation (the probe predicting a relabeled version of itself), proving nothing. The oracle must come from an independent channel — held-out human/LLM-judge joint adjudication, or a held-out high-capacity judge model — so that "calibrated d̂ predicts joint failure" is a real test, not a tautology. Compounding this, the **underlying RMs may be miscalibrated or reward-hacked** (a known RLHF failure): r_h or r_s can be confidently wrong, which is a confounder both for the estimator (garbage-in) and for any oracle that leans on them.

**7. Why naive approaches fail.** [conjectured]
- **A single tuned global λ** cannot be simultaneously correct for benign and adversarial prompts — the optimal trade is *prompt-dependent*; one λ is too safety-dominant for benign prompts (→ over-refusal) and too helpfulness-dominant for adversarial ones (→ unsafe-compliance). This is the confound, restated as a failure mode.
- **Thresholding the raw RM-score gap (uncalibrated)** conflates the **scale/temperature differences** between r_h and r_s — the two RMs are trained separately and their logits are not on a common scale, so a raw |r_h − r_s| threshold mixes calibration artifacts with real conflict.
- **Abstaining on predictive uncertainty alone** (entropy/ensemble variance) ignores the **conflict structure**: a prompt can be low-uncertainty yet high-conflict (both RMs confident, in opposite directions) — exactly the dangerous case — and uncertainty-only abstention will pass it through.

**Why it is nonetheless tractable.** [provable-by-construction] The boundary cases give built-in do-no-harm anchors and free baselines: λ̂≡λ recovers fixed-λ RLHF exactly; disagreement→0 recovers standard decoding; refuse-thresh→∞/→0 give always-answer/always-refuse; disabling clarify+safe-complete reduces to standard selective-refusal; oracle joint labels give a ≤ upper bound. The open RM ecosystem (Safe-RLHF/BeaverTails ships **separate reward + cost models**) supplies a ready two-RM substrate, and the falsifier ladder (Oracle/Placebo/Recovery/Leakage) makes every load-bearing claim refutable. The difficulty is real, but it is *bounded and instrumented*, not open-ended.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

No prior strand is wrong; each solves a **different** slice, and none produces a **per-prompt, label-free, calibrated arbitration on helpfulness-vs-safety RM *disagreement* at inference.** Mapping the five strands shows precisely what each leaves open.

**(1) Multi-objective / two-RM / safe RLHF — combines objectives at TRAINING with a FIXED rule, not per-prompt at inference.** Llama 2 (arXiv:2307.09288) trains two RMs but **combines** them with a fixed/global procedure (and safety-context distillation); it never measures or routes on per-prompt RM disagreement [established]. Safe RLHF (Dai et al. 2023, arXiv:2310.12773) casts safety as a **constrained/Lagrangian** optimization with a cost model — a principled but **global, training-time** tradeoff, solved once for the whole distribution, not arbitrated per prompt at inference [established]. Constitutional AI (Bai et al. 2022, arXiv:2212.08073) and the Anthropic helpful+harmless (HH) program (Bai et al. 2022, arXiv:2204.05862; building on InstructGPT, Ouyang et al. 2022, arXiv:2203.02155) bake the helpful/harmless balance into the **training signal**. All of these fix the tradeoff *before* deployment; ARBITER's object — the residual **per-prompt** mis-specification of any single global rule — is by construction invisible to them.

**(2) Helpfulness-safety tradeoff & over-refusal benchmarks — MEASURE the problem in aggregate, don't ARBITRATE per-prompt.** XSTest (arXiv:2308.01263), OR-Bench (arXiv:2405.20947), and the broader exaggerated-safety literature **document** benign over-refusal [established]; HarmBench (arXiv:2402.04249) and red-team suites document unsafe-compliance. These are **diagnostic instruments**: they quantify *that* a model over- or under-refuses in aggregate, on **disjoint** benign-vs-adversarial sets, but they neither estimate a per-prompt conflict signal nor make a routing decision. Crucially, because the benchmarks are disjoint, **the joint helpful∧safe frontier on the same prompts is never measured** — the field has the two marginals but not the conjunction.

**(3) Selective prediction / abstention / conformal methods for LLMs — abstain on FACTUAL error or uncertainty, not on RM CONFLICT.** Selective prediction (Chow 1970; El-Yaniv & Wiener, JMLR 2010, UNVERIFIED ID; Geifman & El-Yaniv, NeurIPS 2017, UNVERIFIED ID) and conformal prediction (Vovk et al. 2005) abstain when **predictive uncertainty / risk of being wrong** is high [established]. Transposed to LLMs, these methods abstain on **hallucination / factual error** likelihood. ARBITER abstains (or clarifies / safe-completes) on a **different axis entirely**: the *conflict between two value heads* on a prompt that may be perfectly answerable and factually easy — a benign request a safety RM wrongly flags, or an adversarial request a helpfulness RM wrongly rewards. Standard selective prediction has no notion of two-reward-model disagreement as the abstention trigger.

**(4) Reward-model uncertainty / ensembles / reward hacking — quantify RM NOISE, not helpfulness-vs-safety DISAGREEMENT as a deployment signal.** Work on reward-model ensembles and uncertainty (e.g., Coste et al. 2023 and Eisenstein et al. 2023 on reward ensembles to mitigate over-optimization; both UNVERIFIED IDs) treats RM variance as **noise within a single objective** to be averaged down or penalized to curb reward hacking [established]. ARBITER's signal is **structured cross-objective disagreement** — sign/margin disconcordance and rank inversion **between** the helpfulness RM and the safety RM — interpreted not as noise to suppress but as an **actionable per-prompt routing signal**. The framing inversion (disagreement-as-information, not disagreement-as-error) is the conceptual move prior ensemble work did not make.

**(5) Inference-time / decoding-time alignment & multi-objective decoding — tune a GLOBAL mixture, not a per-prompt CALIBRATED arbitration.** Rewarded soups / MORLHF (Rame et al. 2023, arXiv:2306.04488) interpolate weights fine-tuned on diverse rewards to trace a **Pareto front**, and controlled decoding (Mudgal et al. 2023, arXiv:2310.17022) steers generation toward a reward at inference [established]. Both expose a **global mixing coefficient** the operator sets once; neither **estimates that coefficient per-prompt from a measured conflict signal**, and neither **calibrates** the decision on a held-out oracle joint-label subset. ARBITER replaces the global mixture with a **measured, calibrated, per-prompt arbitration field λ̂(x)** plus a discrete action set — recovering the global-mixture methods exactly as the constant-field boundary case [provable-by-construction], and going beyond them only where per-prompt disagreement is informative.

**Barriers that kept it unsolved.** (a) **Alignment is reported in aggregate marginals** — the two-average certification is the cultural default, so the per-prompt joint frontier was never the headline. (b) **No per-prompt JOINT helpful∧safe labels at deployment** — there is no cheap oracle telling you, online, whether a response is both helpful and safe, so a per-prompt arbitration signal seemed unbuildable; ARBITER's answer is a **label-free disagreement estimate calibrated on a small held-out oracle subset**, oracle labels used only in calibration. (c) **Disagreement was treated as RM noise to be averaged out**, not as a deployment routing signal (see strand 4). (d) **Helpfulness and safety benchmarks are disjoint by construction**, so the joint frontier — and the contested region where both harms live — is structurally never measured.

**Differentiation from grounding-gated selective generation.** A closely adjacent line of work — a prior grounding-gated selective-generation approach (the Helpfulness–Compliance Gap line of work) — gates generation on **external factual grounding / claim↔evidence compliance**: the signal is *claim vs. retrieved evidence*, and the question is "is this answer grounded/compliant?". ARBITER's signal is categorically different: **internal RM↔RM disagreement within the alignment system itself** — *helpfulness reward vs. safety reward on the same prompt* — with **no retrieval, no external evidence, no factual grounding**. ARBITER asks "do the model's own two value heads *conflict* on this prompt?". The two are orthogonal: a perfectly grounded, factually compliant answer can still sit in ARBITER's high-disagreement region (a benign prompt the safety RM over-flags), and a low-grounding answer can sit in a zero-disagreement region. Because the trigger signals differ in kind (external grounding vs. internal reward conflict), ARBITER's novelty does **not** collapse into grounding-gated selective generation even though both are selective/routing methods.

**Net positioning.** Each strand is a necessary ingredient and none is the dish: training-time combiners fix a global tradeoff (1), benchmarks measure marginals (2), selective prediction abstains on the wrong axis (3), ensembles suppress same-objective noise (4), and decoding-time methods tune a global mixture (5). ARBITER is positioned as their **inference-time, per-prompt, calibrated synthesis on the helpfulness↔safety disagreement axis**, evaluated on the **joint** risk–coverage curve that strand (2) never reports.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

**System under study.** [established] Given (a) a base RLHF chat model π (open: **Llama-2-Chat**, **Llama-3-Instruct**, **Mistral-Instruct**, or **Tulu**), (b) a frozen **helpfulness RM** r_h, and (c) a frozen **safety RM / cost model** r_s. The cleanest substrate is **PKU-Alignment Safe-RLHF / BeaverTails**, which ships a **separate reward model and a separate cost (safety) model** — exactly the two-RM design Llama 2 advertises. Where a dedicated safety RM is unavailable, train r_h and r_s from the **HH-RLHF helpful and harmless splits**, or use open RMs (OpenAssistant reward models, Ray2333 reward models). The comparator throughout is the **fixed-λ RLHF policy** — same π, same two RMs, same decoding — **never** an unaligned base model and never the best-of-the-two-marginals. [Q1↔Q5 invariant]

**Step 1 — GT-free per-prompt disagreement signal d(x).** [designed] For each prompt x, sample n candidate continuations from π and compute a **calibrated combiner** of four ground-truth-free signals, each capturing a distinct facet of helpfulness↔safety tension:
- **(i) Rank-disagreement between the two RMs** [designed] — rank the n candidates by r_h and by r_s; measure **Kendall-τ / Spearman rank-inversion** between the two orderings, plus per-candidate **sign/margin disconcordance** (does the r_h-preferred response score low on r_s?). This is the core conflict signal and is **scale-robust** by construction (ranks, not raw logits), addressing the scale-mismatch failure of raw-gap thresholding (Q3.7).
- **(ii) RM ensemble / epistemic uncertainty** [designed] — variance across an RM ensemble (or MC-dropout) of r_h and r_s, capturing how *reliable* each RM's verdict is on x.
- **(iii) Decoding-level instability** [designed] — dispersion of safety scores and helpfulness scores across the n samples (response variance, safety-score dispersion): high instability flags prompts where the model's behavior is not robust.
- **(iv) Margin to each RM's decision boundary** [designed] — distance of the chosen response to r_h's and to r_s's refuse/accept boundary; small joint margin = contested.

These are combined by a **calibrator h** (logistic or **isotonic regression**; cite Guo et al. 2017, arXiv:1706.04599, for ECE/temperature/isotonic calibration) fit on a **small held-out ORACLE subset** where per-prompt joint helpful∧safe labels exist, producing a **calibrated** d̂(x) ≈ P[joint-failure | signals], deployed where oracle labels do not exist. [Q3.2 — calibration, not raw signal; Q3.6 — oracle distinct from the cheap RM-gap.] Because Q3.2 identifies **calibration drift across {benign, adversarial, jailbreak}** as the dominant failure of a single global calibrator, h is fit as a **regime-stratified (Mondrian) per-regime calibrator** — a separate calibration map per prompt-regime, with the regime inferred by a lightweight prompt-router and the global calibrator retained as a fallback for unrecognized regimes. The per-regime-vs-global calibrator choice is itself ablated and audited via an explicit calibration-transfer test (below).

**Step 2 — Arbitration policy (per-prompt action / λ̂(x)).** [designed] Map calibrated d̂(x) to a per-prompt action via asymmetric-loss-tuned thresholds: **low conflict ⇒ answer** (standard decoding); **moderate conflict ⇒ safe-complete** (safety-weighted decoding, or **best-of-n re-ranked by a joint score** combining r_h and r_s); **high conflict + recoverable (benign-ambiguous) ⇒ clarify** (ask a disambiguating question); **high conflict + adversarial ⇒ refuse-with-reason** (accountable refusal). Equivalently this is a **per-prompt arbitration field λ̂(x)** replacing the global λ. Sweeping the thresholds traces the joint helpful∧safe **risk–coverage curve**; the threshold objective encodes the **asymmetric loss** (unsafe-compliance penalized more than over-refusal) per Q3.3.

**Datasets (real).** [established] *Helpfulness:* Anthropic **HH-RLHF** (helpful split), **AlpacaEval**, **MT-Bench**. *Over-refusal / exaggerated-safety (benign):* **XSTest**, **OR-Bench**, OKTest. *Safety / adversarial:* **AdvBench**, **HarmBench**, Anthropic **red-team**, HH-RLHF (harmless split), and **Safe-RLHF / BeaverTails** (PKU-Alignment) — which ships the separate reward + cost models, ideal for the two-RM setup. *Jailbreak stress:* jailbreak prompt sets (e.g., DAN-style / GCG-transfer suites) for the distribution-shift transfer test (Q3.2).

**Models / RMs.** [established] Base: Llama-2-Chat, Llama-3-Instruct, Mistral-Instruct, Tulu. RMs: Safe-RLHF **beaver reward + cost** models; OpenAssistant / Ray2333 reward models; or r_h, r_s trained from HH helpful/harmless splits.

**Metrics.** [target-measured] **JOINT helpful∧safe success rate** (the headline; the conjunction, never the two marginals separately) [Q1↔Q5 invariant]; **over-refusal rate** on benign (XSTest / OR-Bench); **attack-success / unsafe-compliance rate** on adversarial (HarmBench / AdvBench); **risk–coverage AURC** and **joint-success@coverage** on the joint objective; **RM-disagreement→error AUROC** (does d̂ predict joint failure above chance?); **calibration ECE / Brier** of d̂ reported **per-regime AND cross-regime** — explicitly the **calibration-transfer metric** (fit on benign → test on adversarial/jailbreak, and vice-versa) that quantifies the Q3.2 drift; **win-rate vs baselines** via LLM-judge + human-style rubric, with **disaggregated reporting** across harm categories.

**Baselines & boundary-case reductions (free baselines).** [provable-by-construction unless noted] **fixed-λ RLHF** (λ̂≡λ) — the primary comparator; **best global-λ sweep** [target-measured] — the strongest single-λ competitor; **uncalibrated RM-gap threshold** — isolates the value of calibration; **uncertainty-only abstention** — isolates the value of conflict structure (Q3.7); **always-answer** (refuse-thresh→∞) and **always-refuse** (refuse-thresh→0); **oracle-label ceiling** (substitute GT joint labels for d̂) — reported as a **≤ upper bound**, never an achievable equality. **Ablations:** drop each signal (i)–(iv) individually; calibrator on/off; **per-regime (Mondrian) vs global calibrator**; symmetric vs asymmetric loss; **clarify + safe-complete disabled ⇒ plain selective-refusal** (isolates the value of the richer action set).

**Falsifier ladder (centerpiece).** **ORACLE** [provable-by-construction] — replace d̂ with GT per-prompt joint helpful∧safe labels ⇒ **ceiling** (≤-bound) on achievable joint risk–coverage; the oracle labeling scheme is **DISTINCT from the cheap RM-gap signal** (independent human / held-out high-capacity judge adjudication) so the "d̂ predicts joint failure" test cannot be passed by trivial probe↔oracle self-correlation [Q3.6]. **PLACEBO (central falsifier)** [designed] — a **matched-rate, matched-compute RANDOM arbitration field**, shuffled **CONSISTENTLY across EVERY term it feeds** (estimator d(x), all routing thresholds, action selection, AND calibrator h) **MUST NOT** improve joint AURC; if a rate-matched random field reproduces the gains, the improvement comes from a changed refusal/clarify RATE, not per-prompt RM-disagreement signal [Q3.5]. **RECOVERY** [provable-by-construction] — λ̂≡λ reproduces the fixed-λ RLHF policy **exactly** (within decoding tolerance). **LEAKAGE** [designed] — **no** per-prompt safety/helpfulness GT labels at inference; calibration uses **only** a disjoint held-out oracle subset; benign/adversarial eval prompts are **disjoint** from the calibration set; audit that estimator inputs (RM scores/logits/internal features) carry no test-set or label leakage. **Do-no-harm gate** [provable-by-construction in the collapse regime] — in the **low-disagreement regime** (Var(d̂) < ε), ARBITER must **not underperform** standard decoding; informative-regime gain is reported as **empirically verified, not guaranteed**, and as **"≥ baseline − ε" non-inferiority** (pre-registered ε) on **each** axis — both over-refusal AND unsafe-compliance must reduce or be non-inferior; trading one for the other **fails** the contract [Q1↔Q5 invariant].

**Expected outcomes.** [target-measured / conjectured] ARBITER traces a joint helpful∧safe risk–coverage curve **bounded-above-by the oracle ceiling and predicted to dominate** the fixed-λ RLHF curve; **simultaneous** reduction of over-refusal (XSTest / OR-Bench) **AND** unsafe-compliance (HarmBench) at **matched coverage**; the **placebo shows no gain** (joint AURC not improved); calibrated d̂ **predicts** joint failure above chance (RM-disagreement→error AUROC > 0.5; ECE/Brier improved over the uncalibrated gap, including under cross-regime transfer).

**Responsible-AI scope.** [standing caveats] (R1) A measured improvement over fixed-λ on the evaluated benchmarks is **not** a safety certification and does **not** bound failure under arbitrary distribution shift. (R2) The disagreement estimate d̂(x) is a **protected internal signal** with dual-use risk — the same field that lowers over-refusal can be inverted by an adversary to route harmful prompts to "answer," so d̂ must **not** be exposed via APIs or model outputs. (R3) **All** metrics are disaggregated across the harm taxonomy (hate, self-harm, weapons, privacy, etc.) and available subgroups; aggregate gains must not mask high-severity category regressions. (R4) Claims are scoped to **English and the evaluated harm taxonomy** (per the Llama 2 source); non-English and out-of-taxonomy behavior is uncharacterized and needs separate evaluation. (R5) Every refuse-with-reason is coupled to a **logged, auditable human-review escalation path** (prompt hash, action, timestamp) with user recourse — no silent terminal refusals. (R6) Oracle/red-team data are handled under documented **governance** — DUA, no retained PII, version-locked taxonomy, BeaverTails/HarmBench under license, and human joint-labels collected under IRB-equivalent review.

**Verified vs. unverified citations.** Directly fetched and title-confirmed (arXiv IDs asserted): Llama 2 (Touvron et al. 2023, 2307.09288); InstructGPT (Ouyang et al. 2022, 2203.02155); Anthropic HH-RLHF (Bai et al. 2022, 2204.05862); Constitutional AI (Bai et al. 2022, 2212.08073); Safe RLHF (Dai et al. 2023, 2310.12773); Rewarded soups (Rame et al. 2023, 2306.04488); XSTest (Röttger et al. 2023, 2308.01263); OR-Bench (Cui et al. 2024, 2405.20947); Controlled Decoding (Mudgal et al. 2023, 2310.17022); HarmBench (Mazeika et al. 2024, 2402.04249); calibration (Guo et al. 2017, 1706.04599).
